content.text: Feature flags are the primary mechanism for developing new agent capabilities safely in a continuous delivery environment. By wrapping experimental capabilities in a flag, teams can merge code to the main branch before the capability is ready for production users, test it with a controlled cohort, and roll it back instantly if problems emerge—all without requiring a code deployment. In agentic AI systems, where a new capability may involve a new model, new tools, and new prompt templates simultaneously, feature flags provide the control plane that makes incremental, observable capability development possible.

Feature flags (also called feature toggles or feature switches) are runtime configuration values that control which code paths or capabilities are active for which users. In the context of agent capability development, a feature flag gates an entire capability bundle: the new prompt version, the new model, the new tool set, and the new routing logic that together implement a new agent capability. Users not in the flag's target population receive the existing stable agent; users in the target population receive the new experimental capability.

For agentic AI systems, flags have a richer set of targeting dimensions than typical web feature flags. In addition to user cohort and percentage rollout, agent capability flags can target by task type (enable the new web browsing capability only for research queries), by session risk level (disable experimental tools for high-stakes financial transactions), or by model availability (enable the new capability only when a specific model is available in the routing layer).

Traditional development practice is to develop a feature in a branch, merge to main when complete, and deploy. In agentic AI systems, this approach creates a dangerous gap: by the time the capability is 'complete' enough to deploy, the team has invested weeks in development with no production feedback. If the capability has a behavioral issue in production (the new model is slower, the new tools fail in unexpected ways, the new prompt produces worse output on tail queries), the only option is a rollback deploy.

Feature flags solve this by decoupling code merge from capability activation. The new code is merged to main as soon as it is technically functional, but the flag keeps it inactive for production users. The team can then activate it for internal users, gather production data, and iterate—all without blocking other features or maintaining a long-lived branch.
Trunk-based development: new capability code merges to main immediately, eliminating long-lived feature branches.
Production testing before launch: the capability receives real production traffic from internal users before any external exposure.
Instant rollback: if a capability has issues, the flag is turned off in seconds without a code deployment or rollback.
Cohort-based testing: the capability can be tested with specific user segments (power users, internal team, specific geographies) before broad rollout.
Decoupled release cadence: the capability can be merged anytime but activated on the team's schedule, independent of the deploy cadence.
A/B comparison: with two flags (old capability and new capability), the team can run a controlled experiment comparing behavioral metrics between cohorts.
Flag debt: flags that are not cleaned up after a capability is fully launched accumulate as dead code paths that complicate maintenance.
Testing complexity: with N flags each having two states, there are 2^N possible code path combinations; testing all of them is infeasible.
Evaluation across cohorts: metrics from flagged and unflagged users must be correctly attributed to avoid mixing the populations in evaluation dashboards.
Cognitive overhead: developers must reason about which code path is active for each user, adding mental load.
State management: if a new capability changes data schema or state format, users who switch between flag states may experience inconsistency.

A feature flag evaluation happens at request time. The agent routing layer receives a user request, constructs a flag context (user ID, session attributes, request metadata), and evaluates the flag to determine which capability bundle to activate. The evaluation typically uses a flag management service (LaunchDarkly, Unleash, Flagsmith) that returns a variant identifier for each configured flag.

For agent capabilities, the flag evaluation result selects the capability bundle: which prompt version to load, which model to use, which tool set to make available, and which orchestration logic to execute. This selection is made at the start of the request and is consistent for the entire duration of the agent's execution—a single agent session does not switch capabilities mid-task.

attrs.alt: Feature flag architecture for agent capabilities showing A/B routing between stable and experimental agents

content.text: The flag-gated capability architecture has three layers: the flag evaluation layer (which determines which capability bundle is active for this request), the capability bundle (which contains the prompt version, model configuration, tool set, and orchestration logic), and the shared infrastructure (LLM client, tool execution environment, logging) that both capability bundles use. The flag evaluation is the only difference in the code path; everything below it is standard agent execution.
When adding a new tool to the agent's tool set that has not been evaluated in production.
When upgrading to a new model version and wanting to validate behavior before switching all users.
When introducing a new prompt template that changes the agent's capabilities or behavior.
When developing a new agentic workflow (e.g., adding web search to a previously search-free agent).
Whenever a new capability involves AI behavior that is difficult to fully validate in pre-production evaluation.

# agent/capability_router.py
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
from typing import Any

import ldclient  # LaunchDarkly Python SDK
from ldclient.config import Config

# Initialize LaunchDarkly client
ldclient.set_config(Config(sdk_key="your-sdk-key"))
ld = ldclient.get()

class CapabilityVariant(str, Enum):
    STABLE = "stable"
    EXPERIMENTAL = "experimental"

@dataclass
class CapabilityBundle:
    prompt_version: str
    model: str
    tools: list[str]
    max_tokens: int
    temperature: float

BUNDLES: dict[CapabilityVariant, CapabilityBundle] = {
    CapabilityVariant.STABLE: CapabilityBundle(
        prompt_version="prompts/agent/base-v1.4",
        model="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
        tools=["web_search", "calculator"],
        max_tokens=2048,
        temperature=0.3,
    ),
    CapabilityVariant.EXPERIMENTAL: CapabilityBundle(
        prompt_version="prompts/agent/multimodal-v2.0",
        model="claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219",
        tools=["web_search", "calculator", "browser", "code_execution"],
        max_tokens=4096,
        temperature=0.2,
    ),
}

def get_capability_bundle(user_id: str, user_context: dict) -> CapabilityBundle:
    """Evaluate feature flag and return the appropriate capability bundle."""
    flag_context = ldclient.Context.builder(user_id)\
        .set("email", user_context.get("email", ""))\
        .set("plan", user_context.get("plan", "free"))\
        .set("internal", user_context.get("internal", False))\
        .build()
    
    variant = ld.variation(
        "agent-multimodal-capability",
        flag_context,
        default=CapabilityVariant.STABLE,
    )
    bundle = BUNDLES[CapabilityVariant(variant)]
    # Log which variant this user received for evaluation attribution
    log_capability_assignment(user_id, variant, bundle)
    return bundle

def log_capability_assignment(user_id: str, variant: str, bundle: CapabilityBundle):
    """Emit a structured log for evaluation attribution."""
    import logging
    logging.info(
        "capability_assigned",
        extra={
            "user_id": user_id,
            "flag": "agent-multimodal-capability",
            "variant": variant,
            "prompt_version": bundle.prompt_version,
            "model": bundle.model,
        }
    )

# agent/run_agent.py
import anthropic
from agent.capability_router import get_capability_bundle
from agent.prompt_registry import PromptRegistry
from agent.tool_registry import ToolRegistry

registry = PromptRegistry()
tools = ToolRegistry()

async def run_agent(user_id: str, user_context: dict, user_message: str) -> str:
    """Run the agent with the capability bundle determined by feature flags."""
    # 1. Evaluate flag to get capability bundle
    bundle = get_capability_bundle(user_id, user_context)
    
    # 2. Load prompt for this capability variant
    system_prompt = registry.render(bundle.prompt_version, user_context=user_context)
    
    # 3. Get tool definitions for this capability variant
    tool_defs = tools.get_definitions(bundle.tools)
    
    # 4. Execute agent with capability bundle parameters
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": user_message}]
    
    while True:  # agentic loop
        response = client.messages.create(
            model=bundle.model,
            max_tokens=bundle.max_tokens,
            temperature=bundle.temperature,
            system=system_prompt,
            tools=tool_defs,
            messages=messages,
        )
        
        if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
            return response.content[0].text
        elif response.stop_reason == "tool_use":
            # Execute tools and continue (implementation omitted for brevity)
            messages = await handle_tool_calls(response, messages, tools)
        else:
            break
    
    return ""  # fallback
Three flag configurations for different agent capability development scenarios:

flag 'agent-browser-tool' is ON for 5% of users. The experimental bundle adds the browser tool to the tool set. Metrics tracked: browser tool call rate, success rate, user satisfaction delta vs. control group.
flag 'agent-model-v2' routes 10% of requests to claude-3-7-sonnet while 90% continue on claude-3-5-sonnet. Metrics tracked: accuracy, latency p99, cost per request, refusal rate.

flag 'agent-prompt-v2' activates a completely redesigned prompt for internal users only (targeting by 'internal=true' attribute). After 2 weeks of internal use, evaluation shows 15% accuracy improvement; flag expanded to 5% of production users.

A coding assistant team was building a new multi-file editing capability for their AI agent. The new capability required a new prompt, a new tool (file_write), and a larger context window. Instead of a feature branch, they merged the capability code on day 3 of development, behind a 'coding-multi-file' flag set to OFF for all users.

Over three weeks, they gradually enabled the flag for: internal engineers (days 1-7), then 1% of external beta users (days 8-14), then 10% (days 15-18). At 10%, they noticed a 12% increase in session abort rate for multi-file tasks. Investigation revealed the file_write tool was not handling Windows line endings correctly. They fixed the bug while the flag remained at 10%. After the fix, they ramped to 100% over 5 days with no further issues. The entire process used no rollback deploys.
failing to remove flags after a capability is fully launched creates growing test matrix complexity. Set a flag expiry date and create a cleanup ticket at flag creation time.
without logging which variant each user received, evaluation metrics cannot be attributed correctly to flag cohorts.
Bundling multiple capabilities in one flag:
if a single flag controls both a new model and a new prompt, you cannot attribute a metric change to either cause independently.
Forgetting to handle flag-off path in tests:
test suites that only test the flag-on path will fail to catch bugs that affect the stable (flag-off) path after refactoring.
Create one flag per independent capability dimension (model, prompt, tool set) rather than one omnibus flag.
Log the flag variant in every request trace so evaluation metrics can be correctly attributed to flag cohorts.
Set a flag expiry date at creation time and create a cleanup task so flags do not accumulate indefinitely.
Test both flag-on and flag-off code paths in your test suite.
Use the flag name as a commit convention scope: feat(agent-browser-tool): add browser capability.
Define success metrics and failure thresholds before enabling the flag for any users, so the rollout decision is data-driven.

Flag evaluation from a managed service (LaunchDarkly, Unleash) adds 1-5ms of latency per request, which is negligible compared to LLM call latency. For high-throughput systems, use the SDK's streaming updates to keep flag state in memory rather than making a network call per evaluation. Flag SDK clients maintain a local cache that is updated in the background, so flag evaluation is effectively free at request time.

Feature flags improve security by allowing new AI capabilities to be activated selectively. A new capability that has not been security-reviewed can be kept off for all users while development proceeds, then enabled for internal users while security review runs in parallel. The kill switch (setting the flag to 0% activation) is a reliability primitive that must be tested before any production traffic is directed to a new capability.

Production-grade feature flag systems for agentic AI go beyond simple on/off switches. They require scoped evaluation contexts, multi-dimensional targeting rules, and automated lifecycle management to handle the complexity of concurrent capability experiments across diverse user cohorts.

Each flag evaluation must carry a context object that encodes the request's dimensions. In agentic AI, context typically includes the requesting user, the session, the deployment environment, and the active capability bundle. LaunchDarkly and similar SDKs accept a multi-context object so targeting rules can reference any combination of these attributes.
Rather than checking individual flags for each component (model, prompt, tool set), define a

as the unit of evaluation. A bundle is a named, versioned set of component choices gated by a single flag key. This ensures atomic rollout: either all components of the new capability are active together or none are.

# capability-bundles.yaml
# Declarative bundle registry checked into version control

bundles:
  agent-v1-stable:
    model: gpt-4o-2024-05-13
    prompt_template: prompts/v1/document-qa.jinja2
    tools:
      - web_search_v2
      - code_interpreter_v1
    max_tokens: 4096

  agent-v2-experimental:
    model: gpt-4o-2024-08-06
    prompt_template: prompts/v2/document-qa-structured.jinja2
    tools:
      - web_search_v3
      - code_interpreter_v2
      - citation_extractor_v1   # new tool under evaluation
    max_tokens: 8192
    eval_required: true         # gate: eval baseline must pass before beta

Integration tests must cover both the flag-on and flag-off paths. Use a test double for the flag SDK that allows overriding flag values per test case. This lets the CI pipeline validate that both the stable and experimental capability bundles produce correct behaviour without requiring a live flag service.

Every flag evaluation should emit a structured log entry that includes the flag key, the resolved variation, the evaluation context key, and the latency of the evaluation. Aggregate these logs to build a real-time picture of what fraction of traffic each capability variation is serving, which is essential for detecting unintended flag state drift or targeting rule misconfiguration.
Emit evaluation events to your observability pipeline (Datadog, OpenTelemetry) with flag key and variation as dimensions.
Instrument agent response latency and quality metrics split by variation so you can compare capability bundles at a glance.
Set automated alerts for flag evaluation error rates above 0.1%—evaluation failures default to the control variation and skew results silently.
Use flag system dashboards (LaunchDarkly Experimentation, Statsig Pulse) to track statistical significance before graduating a flag from canary to general availability.
Feature flags decouple code merge from capability activation, enabling trunk-based development for agentic AI features.
Gate each independent dimension of a new capability (model, prompt, tool set) with a separate flag to enable precise attribution of metric changes.
Log the flag variant in every request trace so evaluation metrics are correctly attributed to flag cohorts.
Set expiry dates on flags at creation time and enforce cleanup to prevent flag debt accumulation.
The kill switch is a reliability mechanism: always test it before directing production traffic to a new capability.
Define success and failure thresholds before the rollout begins so every expansion decision is data-driven.
